metadata.title: Terraform Module Patterns for Production
metadata.description: Reusable module design patterns used in real-world infrastructure with Terraform and Google Cloud.

tags.label: GCP
tags.description: Google Cloud Platform tutorials and tips

frontMatter.title: Terraform Module Patterns for Production
frontMatter.description: Reusable module design patterns used in real-world infrastructure with Terraform and Google Cloud.

nextItem.title: How I Passed the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer Exam

blogPosts.content:
After managing infrastructure across multiple projects, I’ve distilled some **Terraform module patterns** that keep code clean, reusable, and maintainable.

{/* truncate */}

---

## The Layout Pattern

```
modules/
  gce_instance/
    main.tf
    variables.tf
    outputs.tf
  vpc/
    main.tf
    variables.tf
    outputs.tf
```

Each module:
- Has a **single responsibility**
- Exposes only **intentional inputs** via `variables.tf`
- Returns **predictable outputs** via `outputs.tf`

## Version Pinning

Always pin provider and module versions in production.

```hcl
terraform {
  required_providers {
    google = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}
```

## Using `for_each` Over `count`

Use `for_each` for named resources to avoid reordering issues.

```hcl
variable "subnets" {
  type = map(object({
    cidr = string
    region = string
  }))
}

resource "google_compute_subnetwork" "default" {
  for_each = var.subnets

  name          = each.key
  ip_cidr_range = each.value.cidr
  region        = each.value.region
  network       = google_compute_network.vpc.id
}
```

## Remote State with GCS

Store state remotely to enable team collaboration.

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "gcs" {
    bucket = "my-tf-state-bucket"
    prefix = "prod/infrastructure"
  }
}
```

## Summary

- Use modules for reusable components
- Pin provider versions
- Prefer maps and `for_each` over lists and `count`
- Always use remote state in production

What patterns do you use? Let me know in the comments!

metadata.title: How I Passed the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer Exam
metadata.description: Study tips, resources, and hands-on strategies for passing the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer certification.

tags.label: GCP
tags.description: Google Cloud Platform tutorials and tips

frontMatter.title: How I Passed the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer Exam
frontMatter.description: Study tips, resources, and hands-on strategies for passing the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer certification.

blogPosts.content:
Passing the **Google Cloud Associate Cloud Engineer** exam requires a mix of hands-on practice and focused study. Here are the strategies that worked for me.

{/* truncate */}

---

## Study Resources

- **Official Google Cloud documentation** — read it like a textbook
- **Google Cloud Skills Boost** — Quest: "Prepare for Your Associate Cloud Engineer Journey"
- **Practice exams** — Linux Academy / A Cloud Guru / Coursera

## Key Focus Areas

1. **IAM & Identity**
   - Understand the difference between primitive, predefined, and custom roles
   - Know when to use Service Accounts vs User Accounts

2. **Compute Engines**
   - Know how to create and manage GCE instances
   - Understand startup scripts and metadata

3. **Kubernetes Engine (GKE)**
   - Deploy clusters using Console and `gcloud`
   - Know basic `kubectl` commands

4. **Storage & Databases**
   - Cloud Storage bucket classes and use cases
   - When to use Cloud SQL vs Cloud Spanner vs BigQuery

## Hands-On Practice

Create a mini project: deploy a VM, attach a persistent disk, configure a firewall rule, and host a simple app.

```bash
gcloud compute instances create web-server \
  --image-family=debian-11 \
  --image-project=debian-cloud \
  --machine-type=e2-micro \
  --zone=us-central1-a \
  --tags=http-server
```

## Exam Day Tips

- Flag uncertain questions and return later
- Know the pricing model basics (sustained use discounts, committed use)
- Time management: 2 hours for 50 questions

Good luck!
